standards.name: ISO/IEC 27001:2022
standards.issuing_body: International Organization for Standardization (ISO) / International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)
standards.summary: The leading international standard for information security management systems (ISMS). It specifies requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an ISMS, and includes a set of 93 security controls organized into four themes (Organizational, People, Physical, Technological). Certification is awarded by accredited third-party certification bodies.
key_controls: Organizational controls: policies, roles, responsibilities, supplier security
People controls: screening, training, awareness, disciplinary processes
Physical controls: physical security perimeters, clear desk, equipment security
Technological controls: access control, encryption, malware protection, vulnerability management, logging, monitoring
Business continuity management
Incident management and response

standards.name: ISO/IEC 27701:2019
standards.issuing_body: International Organization for Standardization (ISO) / International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)
standards.summary: A privacy extension to ISO 27001/27002, establishing requirements for a Privacy Information Management System (PIMS). It specifies controls for both data controllers and data processors and is explicitly designed to support compliance with GDPR and other data protection regulations. Organizations holding ISO 27001 can extend it with ISO 27701 certification.
key_controls: Privacy governance, accountability, and roles (Data Protection Officer support)
Data minimization, purpose limitation, and consent management
Data subject rights enablement: access, correction, erasure, portability
Data retention and disposal policies
Privacy risk assessments (PIPIAs / DPIAs)
Cross-border data transfer management
Privacy by Design and by Default controls

standards.name: SOC 2 Type II
standards.issuing_body: American Institute of Certified Public Accountants (AICPA)
standards.summary: An auditing standard for service organizations that defines criteria for managing customer data based on five Trust Service Criteria (TSC): Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy. A Type II report covers a period of time (typically 6–12 months), demonstrating sustained control effectiveness — not just point-in-time design.
key_controls: CC1–CC9: Common Criteria (COSO framework — environment, risk assessment, communications, monitoring)
A1: Availability — uptime SLAs, redundancy, disaster recovery
PI1: Processing Integrity — complete, accurate, timely data processing
C1: Confidentiality — data classification, encryption, access controls
P1–P8: Privacy — AICPA privacy criteria aligning to GAPP (Generally Accepted Privacy Principles)

standards.name: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
standards.issuing_body: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), U.S. Department of Commerce
standards.summary: A voluntary, risk-based framework for managing cybersecurity risk, widely adopted by organizations of all sizes globally. Version 2.0 (2024) adds a new 'Govern' function to emphasize cybersecurity governance and strategy, and expands applicability beyond critical infrastructure to all sectors. The framework uses six core functions: Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover.
key_controls: GV — Govern: Cybersecurity risk governance, strategy, supply chain risk management
ID — Identify: Asset management, risk assessment, risk management strategy
PR — Protect: Access control, awareness training, data security, platform security
DE — Detect: Continuous monitoring, anomaly and event detection
RS — Respond: Incident response planning, communications, analysis, mitigation
RC — Recover: Recovery planning, improvements, communications

standards.name: NIST SP 800-53 Revision 5
standards.issuing_body: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), U.S. Department of Commerce
standards.summary: The most comprehensive catalog of security and privacy controls for federal information systems and organizations. Rev. 5 integrates privacy controls directly into the security control catalog and applies to all types of computing platforms. It is mandatory for U.S. federal agencies and provides the control baseline for FedRAMP (cloud) and FISMA compliance.
key_controls: 20 control families including: Access Control (AC), Audit and Accountability (AU), Configuration Management (CM)
Incident Response (IR), Maintenance (MA), Media Protection (MP), Personnel Security (PS)
Risk Assessment (RA), System and Communications Protection (SC), System and Information Integrity (SI)
Privacy controls: Authority and Purpose (AP), Data Quality and Integrity (DI), Individual Participation (IP)
Three impact baselines: Low, Moderate, and High — over 1,000 controls total

standards.name: ISO 9001:2015
standards.summary: The world's most widely adopted quality management standard, with over 1 million certified organizations globally. ISO 9001 defines requirements for a Quality Management System (QMS) based on principles including customer focus, leadership, process approach, continual improvement, evidence-based decision making, and relationship management. It is industry-agnostic and applicable to any organization.
key_controls: Context of the organization: understanding stakeholder needs and organizational scope
Leadership: management commitment, quality policy, organizational roles
Planning: risk and opportunity management, quality objectives
Support: resources, competence, documented information
Operation: process controls, design and development, supplier management
Performance evaluation: monitoring, measurement, internal audit, management review
Improvement: nonconformity management, corrective action, continual improvement

standards.name: ISO 31000:2018
standards.summary: An international standard providing principles, a framework, and a process for managing risk in any organization. ISO 31000 is not certifiable — it is a guidance document applicable to any decision or activity in any type of organization. It provides a structured approach to risk identification, analysis, evaluation, treatment, monitoring, and communication.
key_controls: Risk management principles: integration, structured, inclusive, dynamic, best available information
Framework: leadership commitment, integration, design, implementation, evaluation, improvement
Process: scope definition, risk assessment (identification, analysis, evaluation), risk treatment
Risk treatment options: avoid, modify (reduce), share (transfer), retain (accept)
Monitoring and review: continuous tracking of risk landscape changes
Communication and consultation: stakeholder engagement throughout the risk process

standards.name: CIS Controls Version 8
standards.summary: A prioritized set of 18 cybersecurity best practices (Controls) with 153 Safeguards, developed by security experts to defend against the most prevalent cyber threats. The controls are grouped into three Implementation Groups (IG1, IG2, IG3) based on organizational resources and risk exposure — IG1 is essential cyber hygiene for all organizations.
key_controls: CIS 1–2: Inventory and control of enterprise assets and software
CIS 3–4: Data protection and secure configuration
CIS 5–6: Account management and access control management
CIS 7–9: Continuous vulnerability management, audit log management, email and web browser protections
CIS 10–13: Malware defenses, data recovery, network infrastructure, network monitoring and defense
CIS 14–18: Security awareness training, service provider management, application software security, incident response, penetration testing

standards.name: Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard v4.0.1
standards.summary: A mandatory security standard for all organizations that store, process, or transmit payment card data. PCI DSS v4.0 (effective March 2024) modernizes requirements with a stronger emphasis on authentication, continuous monitoring, and targeted risk analysis. Non-compliance risks contract termination by card brands (Visa, Mastercard, Amex) and significant financial penalties.
key_controls: Req 1–2: Install and maintain network security controls; apply secure configurations
Req 3–4: Protect stored account data; protect cardholder data with encryption in transit
Req 5–6: Protect all systems against malware; develop and maintain secure systems and software
Req 7–9: Restrict access by business need; identify users and authenticate access; restrict physical access
Req 10–11: Log and monitor access; test security systems and processes regularly
Req 12: Support information security with organizational policies and programs

standards.name: COBIT 2019
standards.summary: A comprehensive framework for IT governance and management of enterprise IT, published by ISACA. COBIT 2019 aligns IT strategy with business objectives, ensuring IT risks are managed effectively and resources are used responsibly. It organizes governance and management objectives into five domains and is widely used by internal audit and board-level IT governance programs.
key_controls: Evaluate, Direct and Monitor (EDM): governance framework, benefits delivery, risk optimization, resource optimization
Align, Plan and Organize (APO): strategy, architecture, innovation, portfolio, budget, HR, risk, security, compliance
Build, Acquire and Implement (BAI): program management, requirements, solutions identification, change management
Deliver, Service and Support (DSS): operations, asset management, configuration, problem management, continuity
Monitor, Evaluate and Assess (MEA): performance, internal controls, compliance with external requirements
